title: Why your house feels cold even with the heating on
description: Heating on but still cold? Draughts, damp air, cold walls and an unbalanced system can make 20°C feel like 16. Here are the cheap fixes that actually work.
blurb: Draughts, damp air and cold surfaces make a heated room feel chilly. The cheap fixes that change how warm 20°C feels.
lede: The thermostat says 20°C, the boiler is running, the radiators are hot, and you are still sitting under a blanket wondering where the heat is going. It is one of the most common complaints in UK homes, and the thermostat is usually telling the truth. The air in the middle of the room really is at 20°C. The problem is that your body does not measure air temperature. It measures how fast it is losing heat, and moving air, damp air and cold surfaces all strip heat from your skin faster, so a room that reads warm can genuinely feel cold. The good news: most of the causes are cheap to fix, and fixing them lets you feel comfortable at a lower thermostat setting, which is where the money comes back.
short_answer: A house that feels cold with the heating on usually has one or more of four problems: moving air from draughts, damp air that pulls heat off your skin, cold walls and windows radiating chill at you, or a heating system that is not actually delivering heat where the thermostat thinks it is. None of these show up on the thermostat, which only reads air temperature in one spot. Draughts and damp are the cheapest to fix, typically under £50, and curing them often lets you drop the thermostat a degree, worth roughly 10% of your heating gas.

sections.h2: Your body does not feel temperature, it feels heat loss
sections.html:
The single idea that explains this whole problem: a thermometer measures the air, but your skin measures the rate at which it loses heat. Two rooms at an identical 20°C can feel several degrees apart depending on three things.

  • Air movement. Still air is a decent insulator and your body warms a thin layer of it next to your skin. A draught keeps sweeping that warm layer away, so you lose heat faster. This is wind chill, happening indoors at a smaller scale. Even a gentle 0.5 metre per second draught can make a room feel 1 to 2°C colder than it reads.
  • Humidity. Damp air conducts heat away from skin more effectively than dry air, and it slows the constant low-level evaporation your body uses to regulate itself. A cool damp room feels distinctly more raw than a dry one at the same reading.
  • Radiant temperature. Your body constantly exchanges radiant heat with the surfaces around it. Sit near a large cold window or an uninsulated external wall and you are beaming body heat at it, which reads as a chill on that side of your body even though the air between you is warm.

As a rough rule, what you feel is close to the average of the air temperature and the average surface temperature around you. A room with 20°C air but 14°C walls and glazing feels like roughly 17°C. That gap is why turning the dial up often fails: you are paying to fix the wrong number.

sections.h2: Draughts: the cheapest problem to find and fix
sections.html:
Draughts do double damage. They physically replace air you have paid to heat with cold outside air, and the movement itself makes you feel colder than the thermostat suggests. An older, leaky house can change over its full volume of air well over once an hour; a well sealed one manages with half that or less.

On a cold, windy day, walk the house with the back of your hand or a smoking incense stick and check the usual suspects: gaps under and around external doors, letterboxes and keyholes, loose or aged window seals, the loft hatch, gaps where pipes and cables pass through walls, gaps between floorboards and under skirting on suspended timber floors, and an open chimney, which acts as a permanent extract fan pulling warm air out of the room. None of these costs much to deal with: self-adhesive seals, brush strips, a letterbox flap and a chimney draught excluder are all pocket-money items, and the Energy Saving Trust puts the saving from professional-standard draught-proofing of windows and doors at around £45 a year, before counting the comfort gain. The full room-by-room method is in our draught-proofing guide.

One caution: seal the gaps you never asked for, not the ventilation you need. Trickle vents, extractor fans and air bricks serving combustion appliances are there on purpose. Block those and you trade a draught for condensation and damp, which makes the cold feeling worse.

sections.h2: Damp air feels colder and costs more to heat
sections.html:
If your home feels cold and slightly clammy, if windows stream with condensation on winter mornings, or if you can smell mustiness, humidity is likely part of your problem. Indoor relative humidity in winter is comfortable somewhere around 40 to 60%. Push above 65 to 70% and the same air temperature feels noticeably colder on skin, and damp also soaks into soft furnishings, plaster and even the air itself, all of which then take more energy to warm.

An ordinary household generates litres of water vapour a day from cooking, showers, breathing and, worst of all, drying laundry on radiators. The cheap defences come first: lids on pans, extractor fans actually switched on, bathroom doors closed while showering, washing dried outdoors or in one closed-off room with a window cracked, and a £10 hygrometer so you know your numbers. Our guide to cheap condensation and mould fixes covers the full list.

If the house still sits above 65% with good habits, a compressor dehumidifier is the right tool, and it is cheaper to run than people assume: a typical 250W machine costs about 6.5p an hour at the current cap, it returns its heat to the room as it works, and drier air feels warmer at the same thermostat setting. The numbers are broken down in our dehumidifier running cost guide. In a damp home a dehumidifier plus a 19°C thermostat frequently feels better than 21°C and wet air, and costs less.

sections.h2: Cold surfaces: why you shiver next to a warm radiator
sections.html:
The radiant half of comfort gets far less attention than air temperature, but it explains some very familiar misery. On a freezing day the inside face of a single-glazed window can drop into single figures, and even older double glazing can sit several degrees below the room. An uninsulated solid brick wall behaves the same way on a larger scale. Sit beside either and one side of your body is radiating heat at a cold surface all evening. The air says 20°C; your left arm disagrees.

You attack this from two directions. Short term, put barriers between you and the cold surfaces: close lined curtains and blinds the moment it gets dark, keep furniture you sit in away from external walls and big windows, fit reflector panels behind radiators on external walls, and put rugs over bare floors above unheated voids. Longer term, the real fix is raising the surface temperatures themselves through insulation and better glazing, which is slower and dearer but permanent. A well insulated room feels warm at a lower air temperature precisely because the walls stop stealing radiant heat from you.

sections.h2: When the heating system itself is the problem
sections.html:
Sometimes the house feels cold because, despite the boiler running, heat is not actually arriving where you live. Run through these checks before blaming the building.

  • Radiators cold at the top: trapped air. A radiator key costs about £2 and bleeding takes minutes per radiator.
  • Radiators hot upstairs or near the boiler, lukewarm in the living room: an unbalanced system. Hot water takes the easy route and the far radiators live off leftovers. Balancing your radiators is a free afternoon job apart from a cheap pipe thermometer.
  • Radiators cold at the bottom and hot at the top: sludge. That needs a chemical clean or a power flush, but spotting it early is free.
  • Radiators blocked by sofas or buried behind full-length curtains: the heat goes into the furniture and the window reveal, not the room.
  • The thermostat in the wrong place: if it lives in a hallway warmed by the kitchen, or in the sun, or directly above a radiator, it switches the boiler off while the rooms you sit in are still cold. The reading is honest; the location is lying.
  • TRVs turned down or stuck: a thermostatic radiator valve left on 2 from last spring quietly caps that room regardless of the main thermostat.

Each of these tempts people to turn the main dial up, heating the rooms that were already fine. Fixing distribution first is almost always cheaper, and the rest of our heating guides cover each job in detail, including dropping the boiler flow temperature once everything is balanced.

sections.h2: What the fixes cost, and what turning the dial up costs instead
sections.html:
Here is the menu, roughly in the order most cold-feeling houses should work through it. Note how cheap the high-impact items are compared with the default response of adding a degree.

FixTypical costWhat it cures
Bleed radiators (radiator key)£2, 30 minutesRadiators cold at the top, rooms slow to heat
Self-adhesive seals, brush strips, letterbox flap£15 to £30 for a whole houseMoving air that makes 20°C feel like 18
Chimney draught excluder£20 to £25Warm air pouring up an unused flue
Balance radiators (pipe thermometer)£15 to £25, one afternoonHot hallway, cold living room
Radiator reflector panels (external walls)£10 to £15 per packHeat soaking into cold outside walls
Hygrometer plus better moisture habitsAbout £10Clammy air, streaming windows
Run a 250W dehumidifier when humidity tops 65%About 6.5p per hourDamp air that feels cold on skin
Turning the thermostat up 1°C insteadRoughly £73 a year, every yearOften nothing, if the real cause is above

Dehumidifier cost at 26.11p per kWh electricity; thermostat figure based on about 10% of a 10,000 kWh-a-year space heating load at 7.33p per kWh gas. Ofgem price cap July to September 2026, checked June 2026.

Read the last row against the rest of the table. One degree on the dial costs more every single year than the entire rest of the list costs once. That is the financial case for fixing the feel of the room rather than the number on the wall.

A house that feels cold with the heating on is rarely lying to you and rarely needs a bigger boiler. It is telling you that air is moving when it should be still, that the air is damp when it should be dry, that surfaces are cold when they should be mild, or that the heat is pooling in the wrong rooms. Work through the list in order: bleed and balance the radiators, hunt down the draughts on a windy day, get humidity under 60%, close the curtains at dusk and pull the sofa off the cold wall. Most of that costs under £50 all in. Done properly, the usual result is a home that finally feels warm at the setting you already use, and quite often at a degree less, which quietly pays you back roughly £73 a year at current cap rates for as long as you live there.

faq.q: Why does my house feel cold at 20°C?
faq.a: Because your body senses heat loss, not air temperature. Draughts sweep warmth off your skin, damp air conducts heat away faster, and cold windows or uninsulated walls absorb radiant heat from your body. Any of these can make 20°C air feel like 16 or 17°C. The thermostat is reading the air correctly; the air is just not the whole story.

faq.q: Does damp make a house feel colder?
faq.a: Yes. Humid air pulls heat from your skin more effectively than dry air, so a damp room feels raw and chilly at a temperature that would feel fine if the air were dry. Indoor humidity above about 65 to 70% in winter is the warning zone. Better extraction, not drying laundry on radiators, and if needed a dehumidifier at roughly 6.5p an hour will usually bring it back to a comfortable 40 to 60%.

faq.q: Should I just turn the thermostat up if I still feel cold?
faq.a: It is the most expensive answer and often does not work. Each extra degree adds roughly 10% to space heating costs, around £73 a year for a typical gas-heated home at July to September 2026 cap rates, and if the real problem is a draught, damp air or a cold radiant surface, the room will still feel chilly at the higher setting. Fix the cause first, then see what setting you actually need.

faq.q: Why is one room always cold when the rest of the house is warm?
faq.a: Usually a distribution fault rather than a building fault. The most common causes are an unbalanced system starving the radiators furthest from the boiler, air trapped in that radiator, a thermostatic radiator valve turned down or stuck, or a radiator blocked by furniture or curtains. Bleeding is free and balancing costs only a cheap pipe thermometer, so check both before considering anything bigger.

faq.q: Can a dehumidifier make a room feel warmer?
faq.a: Yes, in two ways. Drier air takes less heat off your skin, so the same air temperature feels warmer, and a compressor dehumidifier releases the heat it extracts plus the energy it consumes back into the room. At about 250W it costs around 6.5p an hour at the current cap, and in a damp home it often makes a lower thermostat setting comfortable, saving more than it costs to run.
